label: Chinese Revolution
pitch:
The Wuchang Uprising of 1911 toppled 2,000 years of imperial rule, but what followed was even more dramatic: Sun Yat-sen proclaimed a republic, Chiang Kai-shek and Mao Zedong fought a decades-long civil war for China's soul, and The Long March forged the Communist movement that would reshape the world's most populous nation.

🔑 Key Facts:
• The May Fourth Movement of 1919 ignited a nationwide awakening of anti-imperialism and modernization that radicalized an entire generation
• The Nanjing Massacre saw Japanese forces kill an estimated 200,000-300,000 civilians in one of WWII's worst atrocities
• People's Republic Founded in 1949 when Mao declared 'The Chinese people have stood up' from Tiananmen
• The Great Leap Forward caused a famine killing an estimated 15-55 million people, the deadliest man-made disaster in history

🤔 Why It Matters: The Chinese revolution created the political system that governs 1.4 billion people today. The Cultural Revolution's scars, the Communist Party's grip on power, and China's rise as a superpower all trace directly to this turbulent era.

🎲 Wild Card: During the Xi'an Incident, Chiang Kai-shek was kidnapped by his own generals and forced to stop fighting the Communists and unite against Japan — a twist of fate that may have saved the Communist Party from destruction.

spans.label: Republic of China
spans.description: The Xinhai Revolution toppled 2,000 years of imperial rule, but the new republic quickly fractured into warlord territories. Sun Yat-sen's nationalist movement and the founding of the Chinese Communist Party set the stage for decades of revolutionary struggle.

spans.label: Nanjing Decade
spans.description: Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government nominally reunified China and pursued modernization from its capital in Nanjing. Simultaneously, Chiang waged extermination campaigns against the Communists, who survived the Long March to establish a base in Yan'an.

spans.label: Second Sino-Japanese War
spans.description: Japan's full-scale invasion devastated China, killing an estimated 15-20 million Chinese including the Nanjing Massacre's 200,000-300,000 victims. Nationalists and Communists formed a fragile united front, but the war exhausted the Nationalist government while the CCP expanded in the countryside.

spans.label: Chinese Civil War
spans.description: The Nationalists and Communists resumed full-scale civil war after Japan's surrender, with Mao's forces using guerrilla tactics and land reform to win peasant support. By 1949 the Communists had swept the mainland, and Chiang Kai-shek retreated to Taiwan.

spans.label: Mao's China
spans.description: Mao transformed China through radical campaigns including land reform, the Great Leap Forward famine that killed tens of millions, and the Cultural Revolution's decade of political upheaval. China became a nuclear power and opened relations with the U.S., but at enormous human cost.

events.label: Wuchang Uprising
events.description: A military revolt in Wuchang sparked the Xinhai Revolution, leading to the collapse of the Qing dynasty and 2,000 years of imperial rule.

events.label: Sun Yat-sen
events.description: The 'Father of the Nation' founded the Republic of China and developed the Three Principles of the People guiding Chinese nationalism.

events.label: Republic of China Proclaimed
events.description: Sun Yat-sen was inaugurated as provisional president, establishing Asia's first republic after the abdication of the last Qing emperor.

events.label: Yuan Shikai Seizes Power
events.description: Military strongman Yuan Shikai became president, then briefly declared himself emperor before his death plunged China into warlord chaos.

events.label: Warlord Era
events.description: China fragmented into territories ruled by competing military warlords, with constant warfare and political instability.

events.label: New Culture Movement
events.description: Intellectuals led by Chen Duxiu attacked Confucian tradition and promoted science, democracy, and vernacular Chinese writing.

events.label: May Fourth Movement
events.description: Student protests against the Treaty of Versailles ignited a nationwide movement for modernization, anti-imperialism, and cultural reform.

events.label: Chinese Communist Party Founded
events.description: The CCP was founded in Shanghai with Soviet support, beginning as a small group of intellectuals including the young Mao Zedong.

events.label: Lu Xun
events.description: China's greatest modern writer used sharp satire in works like 'The True Story of Ah Q' to critique traditional society and inspire revolution.

events.label: Northern Expedition
events.description: The Nationalist army marched north to defeat the warlords and reunify China under the Kuomintang government.

events.label: Chiang Kai-shek
events.description: Chiang led the Nationalist government, fought Japan, and lost the civil war to the Communists before retreating to Taiwan.

events.label: Shanghai Massacre
events.description: Chiang Kai-shek turned against his Communist allies, massacring thousands of CCP members and union workers in Shanghai.

events.label: Nanjing as National Capital
events.description: The Nationalists established Nanjing as the capital of a nominally reunified China, beginning a decade of modernization efforts.

events.label: Mao Zedong
events.description: Mao led the Communist revolution to victory and ruled the People's Republic, transforming China through radical campaigns at enormous human cost.

events.label: The Long March
events.description: The Red Army's 6,000-mile retreat from Nationalist encirclement became a founding myth of Communist China, with Mao emerging as leader.

events.label: Xi'an Incident
events.description: Chiang Kai-shek was kidnapped by his own generals and forced to form a united front with the Communists against Japan.

events.label: Marco Polo Bridge Incident
events.description: A skirmish near Beijing escalated into full-scale war between China and Japan, beginning eight years of devastating conflict.

events.label: Nanjing Massacre
events.description: Japanese forces killed an estimated 200,000-300,000 civilians and prisoners of war in one of the worst atrocities of World War II.

events.label: Yan'an Soviet Base
events.description: The CCP established its base in Yan'an, developing revolutionary ideology, land reform, and guerrilla tactics that would win the civil war.

events.label: Yan'an Talks on Literature
events.description: Mao's talks established that art and literature must serve the revolution and the masses, defining cultural policy for decades.

events.label: People's Republic Founded
events.description: Mao proclaimed the People's Republic of China from Tiananmen, declaring 'The Chinese people have stood up' after decades of war and humiliation.

events.label: Land Reform Campaign
events.description: The CCP redistributed land from landlords to peasants, executing an estimated one to two million landlords in the process.

events.label: China Enters Korean War
events.description: China sent hundreds of thousands of 'volunteers' to fight UN forces in Korea, establishing the PRC as a military power at enormous cost.

events.label: Hundred Flowers Campaign
events.description: Mao invited intellectuals to criticize the government, then cracked down on those who spoke out in the Anti-Rightist Campaign.

events.label: Great Leap Forward
events.description: Mao's campaign to rapidly industrialize through collectivization caused the Great Chinese Famine, killing an estimated 15-55 million people.

events.label: Sino-Soviet Split
events.description: Ideological and geopolitical tensions caused a dramatic rupture between China and the Soviet Union, reshaping Cold War dynamics.

events.label: Cultural Revolution
events.description: Mao launched a decade of political upheaval, mobilizing Red Guards to purge 'capitalist roaders' and destroy traditional culture.

events.label: Red Guard Movement
events.description: Millions of radicalized students formed Red Guard units, attacking teachers, intellectuals, and cultural heritage sites across China.

events.label: Quotations from Chairman Mao
events.description: The Little Red Book became the most printed book of the 20th century, with over a billion copies distributed as Mao's cult of personality peaked.

events.label: Zhou Enlai
events.description: China's longtime premier served as a moderating force, managing foreign policy and attempting to limit the Cultural Revolution's worst excesses.

events.label: Nixon Visits China
events.description: President Nixon's visit to Beijing opened US-China relations, a dramatic diplomatic realignment that reshaped the Cold War balance of power.

events.label: PRC Takes UN Seat
events.description: The People's Republic replaced Taiwan at the United Nations, gaining the permanent Security Council seat.

events.label: First Chinese Nuclear Test
events.description: China detonated its first atomic bomb, becoming the fifth nuclear power and asserting strategic independence from both superpowers.

events.label: Tiananmen Incident of 1976
events.description: Mourners for Zhou Enlai gathered in Tiananmen Square in a protest that foreshadowed the end of radical Maoism.

events.label: Death of Mao Zedong
events.description: Mao's death ended an era of revolutionary upheaval and opened the way for Deng Xiaoping's economic reforms.

events.label: Soong Sisters
events.description: The three Soong sisters married into China's most powerful families, shaping Republican-era politics, finance, and revolution.

events.label: Barefoot Doctors Program
events.description: China trained millions of rural health workers, dramatically expanding basic medical care and doubling life expectancy within a generation.

events.label: Great Hall of the People
events.description: Built in just ten months as one of the Ten Great Buildings, the Great Hall became the seat of China's legislature on Tiananmen Square.

events.label: Yangtze River Flood Control
events.description: Catastrophic Yangtze floods killed over 30,000 people, prompting massive dam-building and flood control infrastructure projects.

events.label: Simplified Chinese Characters
events.description: The PRC promulgated simplified Chinese characters to boost literacy rates, fundamentally changing the written language.

events.label: Destruction of Religious Sites
events.description: Red Guards destroyed thousands of temples, churches, and mosques during the Cultural Revolution's campaign against the 'Four Olds.'

events.label: State Atheism Enforced
events.description: The CCP promoted atheism and restricted religious practice, nationalizing church properties and expelling foreign missionaries.

events.label: Tongmenghui Founded
events.description: Sun Yat-sen merged rival anti-Qing societies into the Revolutionary Alliance in Tokyo in 1905, the organizational ancestor of the Kuomintang.

events.label: Three Principles of the People
events.description: Sun Yat-sen's program — nationalism, democracy, and people's livelihood — became the founding ideology of Republican China.

events.label: Abdication of the Qing
events.description: The six-year-old Puyi abdicated in February 1912, ending 268 years of Qing rule and 2,000 years of imperial government in China.

events.label: Kuomintang Founded
events.description: Song Jiaoren rebuilt the revolutionary movement as the Kuomintang (Nationalist Party) to contest China's first parliamentary elections.

events.label: Assassination of Song Jiaoren
events.description: The KMT's parliamentary leader, poised to become premier, was shot at Shanghai station in 1913 in a killing widely traced to Yuan Shikai's circle.

events.label: The Second Revolution
events.description: KMT-aligned provinces rose against Yuan Shikai in 1913; the revolt was crushed in months and Sun Yat-sen fled into exile.

events.label: The Twenty-One Demands
events.description: Japan presented secret ultimatum demands in 1915 that would have made China a Japanese protectorate; Yuan's partial acceptance became a lasting national humiliation.

events.label: Yuan Shikai's Empire
events.description: Yuan Shikai had himself proclaimed emperor in late 1915; the scheme collapsed within months under nationwide revolt.

events.label: National Protection War
events.description: Yunnan and southern provinces rose against Yuan's monarchy in 1915-16; his death in June 1916 ended the regime and opened the warlord era.

events.label: The Beiyang Army Splits
events.description: Yuan Shikai's modern Beiyang Army fragmented after his death into rival cliques whose generals carved China into private military domains.

events.label: The Warlord Cliques
events.description: The Zhili, Anhui, and Fengtian cliques fought repeated wars for control of the Beijing government and its foreign-recognized legitimacy.

events.label: Zhang Zuolin and the Fengtian Clique
events.description: The ex-bandit Zhang Zuolin ruled Manchuria as the Fengtian warlord under increasing Japanese influence until his 1928 assassination by Japanese officers.

events.label: The Manchu Restoration Attempt
events.description: The warlord Zhang Xun briefly reinstalled the boy Puyi on the throne for twelve days in July 1917 before being driven out.

events.label: China Enters World War I
events.description: China declared war on Germany in 1917 and sent some 140,000 laborers to the Western Front, expecting a seat at the peace table.

events.label: Beijing versus Guangzhou
events.description: Sun Yat-sen set up a rival southern government in Guangzhou against the warlord-run Beijing regime, splitting China's claimed sovereignty.

events.label: The Treaty-Port Economy
events.description: Foreign-run treaty ports like Shanghai became islands of modern industry, banking, and a Chinese commercial middle class amid a collapsing countryside.

events.label: Yan Fu and Social Darwinism
events.description: Yan Fu's translations of Huxley and Spencer injected 'survival of the fittest' into Chinese thought, framing reform as a struggle for national survival.

events.label: The Rural Crisis
events.description: Tenancy, debt, landlordism, and recurring famine ground down the peasant majority and supplied the social fuel for revolution.

events.label: Missionary Schools and Colleges
events.description: Christian missionary schools, colleges, and hospitals trained a Westernized elite even as anti-imperial feeling turned against them.

events.label: New Youth Magazine
events.description: Chen Duxiu's journal New Youth became the platform of the New Culture Movement, championing 'Mr. Science' and 'Mr. Democracy' against Confucian tradition.

events.label: The Vernacular Language Revolution
events.description: Hu Shi led the push to write in everyday spoken Chinese instead of classical literary language, making mass literacy and a new literature possible.

events.label: The Shandong Decision
events.description: The Versailles peace conference handed Germany's former Shandong concessions to Japan rather than back to China, igniting the May Fourth protests.

events.label: Li Dazhao and Chinese Marxism
events.description: The Peking University librarian Li Dazhao was the first major Chinese intellectual to embrace Bolshevism and mentored the young Mao Zedong.

events.label: The Comintern Comes to China
events.description: Soviet Comintern agents organized scattered Marxist study circles into a disciplined party and steered its early strategy from Moscow.

events.label: The Kuomintang Reorganized
events.description: With Soviet help Sun Yat-sen rebuilt the KMT as a Leninist mass party with a party army, the model both Nationalists and Communists would use.

events.label: The Anti-Christian Movement
events.description: Student nationalists attacked Christianity and mission schools as instruments of imperialism, fusing anti-religion and anti-foreign feeling.

events.label: The Science and Philosophy of Life Debate
events.description: A 1923 public debate over whether science could guide human life crystallized the New Culture faith that science would save China.

events.label: The Woman Question
events.description: May Fourth intellectuals attacked arranged marriage and footbinding and made women's emancipation a central revolutionary theme.

events.label: The Sun-Joffe Manifesto
events.description: Sun Yat-sen and Soviet envoy Adolph Joffe agreed in 1923 on Soviet aid to the KMT, opening the alliance with the Communists.

events.label: Whampoa Military Academy
events.description: The Soviet-funded Whampoa Academy, commanded by Chiang Kai-shek with Zhou Enlai as political head, trained the officer corps of both later armies.

events.label: The First United Front
events.description: Communists joined the KMT as individuals from 1924, a Soviet-brokered alliance to defeat the warlords that ended in betrayal in 1927.

events.label: Death of Sun Yat-sen
events.description: Sun Yat-sen died of cancer in 1925, leaving the KMT without its unifying figure and opening a succession struggle Chiang Kai-shek would win.

events.label: The May Thirtieth Movement
events.description: British police killing protesters in Shanghai in 1925 set off nationwide strikes and boycotts that swelled both KMT and Communist ranks.

events.label: The Canton Coup
events.description: Chiang Kai-shek's 1926 'Zhongshan Incident' purge curbed Communist and Soviet influence inside the KMT while keeping the alliance alive for the campaign north.

events.label: The Peasant Movement
events.description: Communist organizers built peasant associations in the countryside; Mao's 1927 Hunan report argued the peasantry, not the workers, was the revolution's engine.

events.label: The Shanghai Workers' Uprising
events.description: Communist-led workers seized Shanghai for the advancing Nationalist army in March 1927, weeks before Chiang turned on them.

events.label: Mikhail Borodin
events.description: The Comintern's chief agent Mikhail Borodin reorganized the KMT and advised its leadership until expelled after the 1927 split.

events.label: The Collapse of the United Front
events.description: After Chiang's Shanghai purge the rival left-KMT government at Wuhan also expelled the Communists by mid-1927, ending the First United Front.

events.label: Nanchang and the Autumn Harvest Uprising
events.description: Failed Communist revolts at Nanchang, in the Hunan countryside, and at Canton in 1927 pushed the survivors toward rural base-building.

events.label: The Northern Expedition Completed
events.description: Nationalist armies took Beijing in 1928 and won the nominal allegiance of the northern warlords, reunifying China on paper under Nanjing.

events.label: The Jiangxi Soviet
events.description: Mao and Zhu De built a rural Communist state in the Jiangxi mountains, proclaiming the Chinese Soviet Republic in 1931 with Mao as chairman.

events.label: Zhu De and the Red Army
events.description: The defecting officer Zhu De co-founded the Red Army with Mao and developed the guerrilla doctrine of trading space for time.

events.label: The Encirclement Campaigns
events.description: Chiang launched five successive offensives against the Jiangxi Soviet; the first four failed before a blockhouse strategy strangled it in 1934.

events.label: The Mukden Incident
events.description: Japanese officers staged a railway bombing in 1931 as pretext to seize Manchuria, creating the puppet state Manchukuo under the ex-emperor Puyi.

events.label: The Nanjing Decade
events.description: Chiang's Nationalist government built railways, a central bank, and a unified currency while never controlling much of the country it claimed.

events.label: The New Life Movement
events.description: Chiang's 1934 campaign blended Confucian morality, Christianity, and military discipline to remake citizens' behavior from the top down.

events.label: The Futian Incident
events.description: A bloody internal purge in the Jiangxi Soviet in 1930-31 foreshadowed the violence of later Maoist campaigns against suspected enemies within.

events.label: The Fifth Encirclement
events.description: Chiang's blockhouse-and-blockade strategy, advised by German officers, finally broke the Jiangxi Soviet and forced the 1934 breakout.

events.label: The Battle of Xiang River
events.description: The Red Army lost more than half its strength forcing the Xiang River in late 1934, discrediting the leadership that ran the retreat.

events.label: The Zunyi Conference
events.description: At Zunyi in January 1935 Mao Zedong won effective control of the Communist Party and army, the turning point of his rise.

events.label: Crossing the Dadu River
events.description: The contested storming of the chained Luding Bridge over the Dadu River became the central heroic legend of the Long March.

events.label: The Snow Mountains and the Grasslands
events.description: The Red Army crossed glaciated passes and a deadly high marsh, losing thousands to cold, altitude, and starvation.

events.label: The Split with Zhang Guotao
events.description: Mao's column broke from the larger force of his rival Zhang Guotao in 1935; Zhang's army was later wrecked, leaving Mao unchallenged.

events.label: The December 9th Movement
events.description: Beijing students marched in December 1935 demanding resistance to Japan, reviving mass nationalist agitation and pressuring Chiang.

events.label: Red Star Over China
events.description: The American journalist Edgar Snow's reporting from Yan'an gave the Communists a sympathetic global image and a heroic Long March narrative.

events.label: The Second United Front
events.description: After the Xi'an Incident Nationalists and Communists formed an uneasy alliance against Japan that held, barely, through the war.

events.label: The Battle of Shanghai
events.description: Three months of urban fighting in 1937 destroyed Chiang's best divisions but proved China would not surrender quickly.

events.label: The Fall of Wuhan
events.description: After Wuhan fell in 1938 the Nationalist government retreated to Chongqing for a long war of attrition deep in the interior.

events.label: The 1938 Yellow River Flood
events.description: Nationalist forces breached the Yellow River dikes to slow the Japanese, drowning hundreds of thousands of Chinese and displacing millions.

events.label: The Battle of Taierzhuang
events.description: A rare major Chinese victory in 1938 punctured the myth of Japanese invincibility and lifted morale for the long war.

events.label: The Eighth Route Army
events.description: The Communists' renamed Red Army waged rural guerrilla war behind Japanese lines, expanding party-run base areas across the north.

events.label: The Wang Jingwei Regime
events.description: The senior Nationalist Wang Jingwei defected to head a Japanese-sponsored collaborationist government in Nanjing from 1940.

events.label: The Hundred Regiments Offensive
events.description: A large 1940 Communist offensive damaged Japanese infrastructure but drew brutal reprisals and a shift back to careful guerrilla strategy.

events.label: The New Fourth Army Incident
events.description: Nationalist troops ambushed their Communist allies in 1941, effectively ending real cooperation while the United Front survived only on paper.

events.label: The Bombing of Chongqing
events.description: Years of Japanese air raids on the wartime capital killed tens of thousands of civilians in one of history's first sustained terror-bombing campaigns.

events.label: The Burma Road and Foreign Aid
events.description: China's overland lifeline through Burma carried Allied supplies until Japan cut it in 1942, after which aid had to be flown over the Himalayas.

events.label: China Joins the Allies
events.description: After Pearl Harbor China became a formal Allied power; US General Stilwell arrived to run a frustrating advisory mission to Chiang.

events.label: The Yan'an Rectification Movement
events.description: Mao's 1942-44 campaign of study, confession, and purge disciplined the party around his leadership and his version of ideology.

events.label: Mao Zedong Thought
events.description: The 1945 Seventh Party Congress enshrined 'Mao Zedong Thought' in the party constitution, fusing Marxism with Chinese rural revolution.

events.label: The Henan Famine
events.description: Drought, war, and Nationalist grain requisitioning killed an estimated two to three million people in Henan and shattered the regime's rural support.

events.label: The Mass Line and Rent Reduction
events.description: In its base areas the party cut rents and interest instead of seizing land, winning peasant loyalty without alienating the war effort.

events.label: Operation Ichigo
events.description: Japan's massive 1944 offensive smashed Nationalist armies across central and southern China, exposing the regime's wartime decay.

events.label: The Cairo Conference
events.description: Chiang met Roosevelt and Churchill in 1943; the unequal treaties were formally abolished and China was promised great-power status.

events.label: The Dixie Mission
events.description: A 1944 US military and diplomatic mission to Yan'an reported the Communists looked more disciplined and effective than the Nationalists.

events.label: Japan's Surrender
events.description: Japan surrendered in August 1945; a Soviet blitz overran Manchuria and handed its arsenals and a strategic foothold to the Communists.

events.label: Wartime Hyperinflation
events.description: Runaway money-printing to fund the war destroyed savings and the salaried middle class, fatally corroding Nationalist legitimacy.

events.label: The Chongqing Negotiations
events.description: Mao flew to Chongqing in 1945 for US-brokered talks with Chiang; the resulting agreement collapsed within months into open war.

events.label: The Marshall Mission
events.description: US General George Marshall's year-long effort to broker a coalition government failed, and Washington largely disengaged.

events.label: The Civil War Resumes
events.description: Full-scale war returned in 1946; Nationalists held the cities and railways while Communists controlled the countryside and grew.

events.label: The Outline Land Law
events.description: The 1947 land law turned the war into a social revolution, redistributing landlord holdings and binding millions of peasants to the Communist cause.

events.label: The Liaoshen Campaign
events.description: Lin Biao's forces destroyed the Nationalists' best armies in Manchuria in 1948, decisively tipping the war.

events.label: The Huaihai Campaign
events.description: The vast 1948-49 Huaihai battle annihilated the Nationalists' central China armies and opened the road to the Yangtze.

events.label: The Pingjin Campaign
events.description: Beijing surrendered without a fight in early 1949 as the Communists took the north China plain.

events.label: The Gold Yuan Collapse
events.description: A botched 1948 currency reform wiped out remaining urban savings and broke the last middle-class faith in the Nationalist state.

events.label: Crossing the Yangtze
events.description: The Communists forced the Yangtze in April 1949 and took the Nationalist capital Nanjing; the regime fled south, then to Taiwan.

events.label: The Retreat to Taiwan
events.description: Chiang's government, two million followers, the gold reserve, and the imperial art collection withdrew to Taiwan, freezing the civil war unresolved.

events.label: New Democracy and the United Front
events.description: The 1949 political consultative conference let minor parties and non-Communists co-found the new state under firm party control.

events.label: The Common Program
events.description: The provisional 1949 constitution promised a multi-class 'New Democracy' and a mixed economy before the rapid turn to socialism.

events.label: The Sino-Soviet Treaty
events.description: Mao spent two months in Moscow to secure a 1950 alliance and loans from Stalin, anchoring China in the Soviet bloc.

events.label: The Marriage Law of 1950
events.description: The new state banned arranged marriage and concubinage and granted women divorce rights, one of the revolution's most consequential social changes.

events.label: Suppression of Counterrevolutionaries
events.description: A 1950-53 campaign executed hundreds of thousands of alleged former Nationalists, bandits, and 'class enemies' to consolidate the new order.

events.label: The Three-Anti and Five-Anti Campaigns
events.description: Campaigns against corruption and private business in 1951-52 broke the urban capitalist class and brought commerce under state control.

events.label: The Annexation of Tibet
events.description: The PLA entered Tibet in 1950; the 1951 Seventeen Point Agreement formalized Chinese sovereignty over the long-autonomous region.

events.label: The Incorporation of Xinjiang
events.description: The PLA entered Xinjiang in 1949, ending its semi-independence and beginning Han settlement and military colonization of the northwest.

events.label: Thought Reform of Intellectuals
events.description: Universities and professionals were put through study, confession, and self-criticism to remold the old educated class.

events.label: Resist America, Aid Korea
events.description: A vast home-front mobilization around the Korean War welded patriotism to the new regime and justified domestic crackdowns.

events.label: The First Five-Year Plan
events.description: A Soviet-style 1953-57 plan poured investment into heavy industry, building the heavy-industrial core of the new economy.

events.label: The Soviet 156 Projects
events.description: Soviet engineers and blueprints built scores of large factories and trained a generation of Chinese technicians before the 1960 rupture.

events.label: Agricultural Collectivization
events.description: Peasants were pushed from mutual-aid teams into collective farms by 1956, undoing land reform's private plots within a few years.

events.label: The 1954 Constitution
events.description: China's first PRC constitution created the National People's Congress and a formal state structure under party supremacy.

events.label: The Gao Gang Affair
events.description: The 1953-54 purge of the powerful regional leaders Gao Gang and Rao Shushi was the first major elite power struggle of the new state.

events.label: The Socialist Transformation of Industry
events.description: By 1956 nearly all remaining private firms were absorbed into joint state-private enterprises, ending the mixed economy.

events.label: Pinyin and the Common Speech
events.description: The state promoted simplified characters, a standard national spoken language, and the pinyin romanization system to drive mass literacy.

events.label: The De-Stalinization Shock
events.description: Khrushchev's 1956 secret speech against Stalin's cult unsettled Mao and pushed him to seek a distinctively Chinese road to socialism.

events.label: The Anti-Rightist Campaign
events.description: After inviting criticism in the Hundred Flowers, Mao reversed course in 1957 and labeled some 550,000 intellectuals 'rightists', silencing dissent for two decades.

events.label: The People's Communes
events.description: In 1958 the countryside was reorganized into giant communes that pooled land, labor, tools, and even cooking, abolishing private life.

events.label: The Backyard Furnaces
events.description: Tens of millions melted pots and tools in crude village furnaces to hit steel targets, producing mostly useless metal and neglecting the harvest.

events.label: The Sputnik Fields
events.description: Officials reported wildly inflated grain yields; the state then requisitioned grain against the fake figures, leaving villages with nothing.

events.label: The Four Pests Campaign
events.description: A campaign to exterminate sparrows removed a check on insects, worsening the crop collapse — a textbook case of ecological backfire.

events.label: The Lushan Conference
events.description: When Defense Minister Peng Dehuai privately criticized the Leap in 1959, Mao destroyed him, ensuring no official dared report the famine.

events.label: The Second Taiwan Strait Crisis
events.description: Heavy shelling of the Nationalist-held islands of Quemoy in 1958 brought China and the US close to nuclear confrontation.

events.label: The 1959 Tibetan Uprising
events.description: A failed Lhasa revolt in 1959 ended Tibetan autonomy and sent the Dalai Lama into permanent exile in India.

events.label: Mao Steps Back
events.description: Mao gave up the state chairmanship to Liu Shaoqi in 1959, a handover that set up the later clash over the famine and reform.

events.label: The Great Chinese Famine
events.description: The Leap caused history's deadliest famine; scholarly estimates of excess deaths run from roughly 15 to 45 million, with most around 30 million.

events.label: The Xinyang Incident
events.description: In one Henan prefecture roughly a million people starved in 1959-60 while granaries stayed locked, a case that exposed the famine's man-made core.

events.label: The Seven Thousand Cadres Conference
events.description: At a huge 1962 work conference Liu Shaoqi blamed the disaster largely on policy, not weather, openly straining his relationship with Mao.

events.label: The Liu-Deng Recovery
events.description: Liu Shaoqi and Deng Xiaoping rolled back the communes, restored private plots and markets, and pulled the economy back from collapse.

events.label: The Soviet Advisors Withdraw
events.description: Moscow abruptly pulled all advisers and blueprints in 1960, halting hundreds of projects and deepening China's isolation.

events.label: The Sino-Indian War
events.description: A short, sharp 1962 Himalayan border war humiliated India and asserted China's frontier claims while it was still reeling from the famine.

events.label: The Socialist Education Movement
events.description: Mao's 1963-65 rural rectification campaign against 'capitalist' backsliding became a rehearsal for the assault on the party itself.

events.label: Learn from Dazhai
events.description: Mao held up the model brigade Dazhai and the Daqing oilfield as proof that self-reliant socialist zeal could replace expertise and aid.

events.label: The Dismissal of Hai Rui
events.description: A historical play read as a coded defense of the purged Peng Dehuai; its 1965 denunciation was the opening shot of the Cultural Revolution.

events.label: Learn from Lei Feng
events.description: The cult of a selfless model soldier, promoted by Lin Biao from 1963, primed the population for total devotion to Mao.

events.label: Lin Biao and the Mao Cult
events.description: Defense Minister Lin Biao turned the army into the engine of Mao worship and rose as Mao's anointed successor.

events.label: The May 16 Circular
events.description: Mao's 1966 directive declared enemies had infiltrated the party and created the Cultural Revolution Group that ran the purge.

events.label: The Big-Character Posters
events.description: Mass handwritten wall posters, beginning at Peking University in 1966, became the medium of denunciation and factional war.

events.label: The Tiananmen Rallies
events.description: Mao reviewed millions of Red Guards in eight 1966 Tiananmen rallies, unleashing students nationwide against authority.

events.label: The Fall of Liu Shaoqi
events.description: China's head of state was denounced as the 'top capitalist roader', tortured, and left to die in prison in 1969.

events.label: The Wuhan Incident
events.description: A 1967 military-backed revolt against central radicals nearly tipped the Cultural Revolution into open civil war.

events.label: Down to the Countryside
events.description: From 1968 some 17 million urban youth were sent to villages, dispersing the Red Guards and creating a 'lost generation'.

events.label: Cleansing the Class Ranks
events.description: The least-known and deadliest phase, a 1968-69 bureaucratic purge that killed on the order of a million people.

events.label: The Ninth Party Congress
events.description: The 1969 congress declared the Cultural Revolution a victory and wrote Lin Biao into the constitution as Mao's successor.

events.label: The Shanghai January Storm
events.description: Radicals seized the Shanghai government in early 1967; the experiment was reined into army-led 'revolutionary committees'.

events.label: The Zhenbao Island Clash
events.description: A 1969 border battle with the USSR brought the two powers near war and pushed China toward an opening to the United States.

events.label: The Lin Biao Incident
events.description: Mao's chosen heir allegedly plotted a coup and died in a 1971 plane crash fleeing to the USSR, gutting faith in the whole movement.

events.label: Ping-Pong Diplomacy
events.description: A 1971 table-tennis exchange signaled the secret thaw that led to Kissinger's visit and the US opening.

events.label: The Gang of Four
events.description: Jiang Qing and three Shanghai radicals formed the faction that ran the late Cultural Revolution and fought the moderates over the succession.

events.label: Criticize Lin, Criticize Confucius
events.description: A 1973-74 campaign nominally against Lin Biao and Confucius was a coded radical attack on the moderate Zhou Enlai.

events.label: The Return of Deng Xiaoping
events.description: Twice-purged Deng Xiaoping was brought back to run the government and push the Four Modernizations before the radicals ousted him again.

events.label: The Four Modernizations
events.description: Zhou Enlai's 1975 program of modernizing agriculture, industry, defense, and science became the blueprint for the post-Mao era.

events.label: The Tangshan Earthquake
events.description: A 1976 quake killed on the order of a quarter-million people and, by tradition, seemed to foretell the fall of the dynasty as Mao lay dying.

events.label: The Arrest of the Gang of Four
events.description: Weeks after Mao's death Hua Guofeng arrested Jiang Qing and her allies, ending the Cultural Revolution and opening the way to Deng.
